transcript: G M Breakpoint Day 3, let's go! Look at this crowd! Yes! Beautiful faces. How much fun are we having? Is this like filling our hearts with so much joy you can't even comprehend it? How many hugs have we gotten? I've gotten at least a bazillion hugs. It is my favorite time of year and it... this... this is... this is indescribable in every way. We've made it to Day 3, we have a stacked, stacked lineup for you. I have the absolute honor of introducing one of my favorite people in the ecosystem. He is revolutionizing privacy and encryption. You might know him as... as Mr. CEO of Privacy. Yannik, give the biggest round of applause, please.

sources.title: Announcing Stellar X-Ray, Protocol 25
sources.snippet:
Stellar | Announcing Stellar X-Ray, Protocol 25
[](https://stellar.org/)
[Dev Docs](https://developers.stellar.org)
Menu
Developers
# Announcing Stellar X-Ray, Protocol 25
![]()
Author
Bri Wylde
Publishing date
It’s about that time again —a new protocol upgrade is on the horizon for the Stellar network: X-Ray! This upgrade begins laying the groundwork for developers to build configurable, compliance-forward privacy applications using zero-knowledge (ZK) cryptography without compromising the transparency that defines Stellar.
At Meridian 2025, Stellar Development Foundation’s (SDF) Chief Product Officer Tomer Weller outlined the long-term privacy strategy for Stellar (see the presentation[here](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=j36W1LUGGrs)). A core pillar of that strategy is a commitment to invest in critical privacy infrastructure and build the foundational cryptographic capabilities developers need before higher-level privacy solutions can exist.
X-Ray —named after the everyday tool used to show only what needs to be seen and nothing more —represents the first major milestone in that plan. It proposes native support for two important primitives:
* **BN254**, a widely used pairing-friendly elliptic curve, and
* **Poseidon**, a family of hash functions designed specifically for ZK proof systems
## Protocol timeline
* Testnet vote: January 7, 2026
* Mainnet vote: January 22, 2026
Follow progress and preparation instructions through the continuously updated[Protocol X-Ray Upgrade Guide](https://stellar.org/blog/developers/stellar-x-ray-protocol-25-upgrade-guide).
What X-Ray introduces and why it matters
## BN254: connecting Stellar to the broader ZK ecosystem
[CAP-0074: Host functions for BN254](https://github.com/stellar/stellar-protocol/blob/master/core/cap-0074.md)proposes adding native host functions that enable elliptic curve operations on BN254, the elliptic curve standard used across many well-known ZK applications, such as privacy pools, Lighter, Starknet, and ZK Email. While Stellar already supports BLS12-381, a newer pairing-friendly curve introduced in[CAP-0059: Host functions for BLS12-381](https://github.com/stellar/stellar-protocol/blob/master/core/cap-0059.md), BN254 remains the most widely used curve in today’s ZK ecosystem.
Without native Soroban support for BN-254, developers building ZK applications on Stellar have to either rewrite their applications for a different curve or maintain workarounds that add complexity and cost. CAP-0074 removes that friction by introducing[three host functions](https://github.com/stellar/stellar-protocol/blob/master/core/cap-0074.md#new-host-functions-introduced):
* `bn254\_g1\_add`
* `bn254\_g1\_mul`
* `bn254\_multi\_pairing\_check`
These functions enable efficient on-chain verification of zk-SNARK proofs, provide feature parity with Ethereum’s[EIP-196](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-196)and[EIP-197](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-197)precompiles, and make Stellar interoperable with existing ZK tooling and libraries. That means builders currently in EVM environments can easily migrate or extend projects to Stellar.
Supporting both BN254 and BLS12-381 helps enable configurable privacy on Stellar by giving developers the flexibility to choose the curve that best suits their goals.
What X-Ray introduces and why it matters
## Poseidon and Poseidon2: hash functions designed for ZK systems
While BN254 provides the pairing operations for proof verification, you also need an efficient cryptographic hash, which is where Poseidon comes in.
[CAP-0075: Cryptographic Primitives for Poseidon/Poseidon2 Hash Functions](https://github.com/stellar/stellar-protocol/blob/master/core/cap-0075.md)defines host functions that expose the core permutation primitives behind Poseidon and Poseidon2.
These primitives let developers design hash functions that are highly efficient in ZK circuits, addressing a key performance bottleneck in ZK systems. Traditional hashes like SHA-256 are computationally e

sources.title: Ika Announces EdDSA Signatures, Expanding Native Support to Solana, Zcash, Cardano and More - Chainwire
sources.snippet:
Ika Announces EdDSA Signatures, Expanding Native Support to Solana, Zcash, Cardano and More - Chainwire
[](https://chainwire.org/)
[Log In](https://app.chainwire.org/login)
# Ika Announces EdDSA Signatures, Expanding Native Support to Solana, Zcash, Cardano and More - Chainwire
* [Home](https://chainwire.org/)
* [Newsroom](https://chainwire.org/category/newsroom/)
* Ika Announces EdDSA Signatures, Expanding Native Support to Solana, Zcash, Cardano and More
![](https://chainwire.org/wp-content/smush-webp/2025/12/Group_4138_1764775174e7nrJV5R9j.jpg.webp)
**04**Dec
## Ika Announces EdDSA Signatures, Expanding Native Support to Solana, Zcash, Cardano and More
**Zug, Switzerland, December 4th, 2025, Chainwire**
[Ika](https://ika.xyz/), the fastest parallel MPC network, today announced a mainnet upgrade that enables**EdDSA signatures directly from dWallets**, significantly expanding Ika’s native cross-chain coverage to include**Solana, Zcash, Cardano, Stellar, Near**, and other EdDSA-based ecosystems.
This upgrade extends Ika’s core promise:**natively control accounts and assets across chains without bridges, wrapping, custodians, or trusted signers**, now across a broader set of networks where EdDSA is the standard.
> “EdDSA support is a major expansion of what dWallets can control natively,” said **> Omer Sadika
**> , Co-Founder of Ika. “It brings zero-trust, programmable signing to some of the most important ecosystems in crypto, so developers can orchestrate real cross-chain actions with real native assets, governed by Ika dWallet technology.”
**What This Upgrade Enables**
Until now, Ika’s dWallets provided zero-trust signing and programmable control for major ecosystems, including Bitcoin and EVM-compatible networks with ECDSA signatures. With EdDSA enabled at the dWallet signing layer, builders can now create applications that:
* **Control Solana accounts natively**, enabling cross-chain trading, vaults, payments, and automation without wrapped assets.
* **Coordinate Zcash-native actions**under programmable dWallet policies, unlocking zero-trust custody and interoperability for the most prominent privacy-preserving cryptocurrency.
* **Manage Cardano, Stellar, Near accounts**with smart contract-governed access control, enabling organizational treasury workflows and institutional-grade policy enforcement.
In short:**dWallets become natively multi-ecosystem signers**, and enable a coordination layer for policy, composability, and execution logic.
**Native Cross-Chain Control**
Most cross-chain systems rely on bridges, message relays, wrappers, or trusted intermediaries. These approaches introduce risk (custody, exploits, governance capture) and limit composability.
Ika’s model is different:**no bridging of assets is required**. Instead, Ika enables**native signing**via dWallets, where transaction authorization is always enforced under zero-trust assumptions.
With EdDSA enabled, the same architecture now extends to chains where ECDSA is not supported and EdDSA has been adopted instead, dramatically expanding the surface area of what Ika dWallets can safely and directly orchestrate.
**How EdDSA Works in Ika’s Zero-Trust Model**
Ika’s security model is built on its**2PC-MPC**cryptographic scheme.
dWallet signing splits authorization into two independent components:
* **User share:**held and authorized locally by the user
* **Network share:**operated by Ika’s decentralized network of MPC nodes
Both are required to produce a valid signature, and the full private key is never reconstructed. With this mainnet upgrade, that same model now supports**EdDSA signing**, bringing the benefits of Ika’s architecture (zero-trust, decentralization, scalability, sub-second performance, and programmable policy enforcement) to a wider set of chains.
**Why It Matters for Builders and the Ecosystem**
This upgrade expands the design space for applications that are only possible with Ika, including:
**Universal accounts across ecosystems**
One programmab

tags.impact_reason: Day 3 introduction with vague mention of privacy solutions. Headline data suggests broader privacy efforts, but this introduction itself is low impact.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose the 'Dark Pool' as the semantic anchor because it's a direct and powerful visual metaphor for the private, institutional financial activity that Arcium's new standard enables. The contrast between the dark, opaque water and the reflection of the public, glowing Solana data streams perfectly narrates the story of 'private strategies on a public mainnet.' I included the diegetic text 'CONFIDENTIAL' etched into the pool's rim to reinforce the core benefit of the technology in a subtle, architectural way, fitting for an institutional setting.
visual_idea.description: A macro, top-down shot looking directly into the dark pool. The frame is tightly cropped on the pool's edge, where the polished obsidian rim meets the perfectly still, inky black liquid. The water's surface is dominated by the distorted, vibrant reflection of the Solana data grid from above. A single, large transaction is implied by a slow, subtle ripple that warps the reflection, suggesting immense power and value moving just beneath the opaque surface.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A macro, top-down shot, composed for a perfect 1:1 square aspect ratio, looking directly into a dark, profound pool. The frame is tightly cropped on the pool's edge, creating a symmetrical, central focus. The rim, crafted from highly polished obsidian, gleams with sharp, dramatic chiaroscuro highlights and deep shadows, its smooth, reflective surface filling the frame with rich, dark texture. It meets a perfectly still, inky black liquid, a vast expanse of opaque depth. The water's surface is entirely dominated by the distorted, vibrant, holographic reflection of a complex Solana data grid from directly above. Glowing neon lines and intricate patterns of light shimmer and stretch across the black surface. A single, colossal transaction is implied by a slow, subtle, powerful ripple that emanates from the center, gently warping and stretching the holographic reflection, suggesting immense power and immeasurable value moving just beneath the inscrutable, liquid surface. The scene is rendered with stark, cinematic contrast, emphasizing the interplay of light and profound darkness. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, photorealistic, cinematic contrast, volumetric lighting.

editorial.headline: Arcium Unveils Confidential SPL Standard to Unlock Institutional Dark Pools
editorial.standfirst: Yannik Schrade's new 'Encrypted Shared State' primitive eliminates the transparency bottleneck, allowing institutions to execute private strategies on public Solana mainnet.
key_takeaways: Launch of **CSPL (Confidential SPL)**: A new standard enabling native privacy for any existing Solana token without wrapping or bridging.
**Encrypted Shared State**: A breakthrough primitive allowing smart contracts to compute over encrypted data, essential for on-chain dark pools and sealed-bid auctions.
**Institutional Adoption Vector**: Solves critical 'strategy leakage' and front-running risks that have historically kept TradFi market makers off public ledgers.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
For years, the 'transparency paradox' has effectively firewalled institutional capital from public blockchains. While the immutability of a public ledger provides trust, the visibility of that ledger makes it impossible for market makers and funds to execute large-scale strategies without being front-run or revealing proprietary alpha. At Breakpoint Day 3 in Abu Dhabi, **Arcium** CEO **Yannik Schrade** dismantled this barrier, not with a new privacy coin, but with a computational infrastructure layer that fundamentally changes how Solana processes state.

The core of the announcement is the **Confidential SPL (CSPL)** standard, underpinned by Arcium’s 'Encrypted Shared State' technology. Unlike previous privacy solutions that relied on isolating assets (like Zcash) or mixing services (like Tornado Cash), CSPL allows for *programmable* privacy directly on Solana. This means developers can now build smart contracts where the logic is public and verifiable, but the inputs—such as trade size, wallet balance, or liquidation price—remain cryptographically sealed. For the first time, a decentralized exchange can operate a 'Dark Pool' on-chain, matching orders without exposing the order book to the public mempool.

This distinction is critical for the venture and institutional audience. **Privacy is no longer about anonymity; it is about commercial confidentiality.** By enabling 'smart token accounts' that possess no private key and are managed entirely by code, Arcium allows for complex financial instruments—like under-collateralized lending or private payroll—to exist on Solana compliant with business logic. The architecture leverages a **Multiparty Computation (MPC)** network to process these encrypted states, ensuring that no single node ever sees the raw data, maintaining liveness and composability with the broader Solana ecosystem.

The implication for 2026 is a massive repricing of Solana's utility for TradFi. If institutions can now execute compliant, confidential transactions on the world's most performant chain, the argument for private, permissioned blockchains evaporates. Arcium has effectively delivered the 'SSL layer' for Web3, turning privacy from a regulatory liability into a necessary infrastructure for the next trillion dollars of on-chain volume.
